retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Large-volume, watery stools with less than 5 white blood cells (WBCs) per high power field (hpf)hpf likely arise from the small bowel. These stools may test positive for guaiac but are rarely grossly bloody. Potential bacterial pathogens include Bacillus species, Staphylococcus aureus , Escherichia coli , Vibrio cholerae , Clostridium perfringens , Vibrio species, and Listeria monocytogenes . Small-volume stools with visible mucus or blood and possibly more than 10 WBCs/hpf likely originate from the large bowel. Possible culprits include Escherichia coli , Shigella , Salmonella , Campylobacter , Yersinia , Aeromonas , Plesiomonas species, and Clostridium difficile . [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba coli Infection -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may rarely show gastrointestinal symptoms such as loose stools, colicky abdominal pain, and flatulence.

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba coli Infection -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with Entamoeba coli infestation remain asymptomatic, and often, the protozoan is discovered incidentally. [26] It is important in those patients with diarrhea symptoms to obtain a thorough history and physical exam. Patients may rarely show gastrointestinal symptoms such as loose stools, colicky abdominal pain, and flatulence. [27] Some patients can complain of gastritis, indigestion, dyspepsia, or hyperacidity if the amoeba is in large amounts in the gastrointestinal tract. Stools are usually non-bloody and without mucus. Symptoms may persist for a long time; however, they are generalized and nonspecific for Entamoeba coli .

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Gastroenteritis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common history findings for a patient with gastroenteritis are as follows: Nausea Diarrhea (watery or bloody in dysentery) Vomiting Abdominal pain Fever (suggests an invasive organism as the cause)

retrieved_docs.title: Amebiasis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Colitis caused by E. coli , Yersinia ,  or Campylobacter Pericarditis Perforated bowel Diverticulitis Hepatitis A Cholecystitis Shigellosis/salmonellosis

retrieved_docs.title: Giardia Lamblia Enteritis (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Acute giardia enteritis symptoms include the onset of frequent diarrhea.  One study noted maximal stools of 8.5 per day as a median when the number of maximal stools was analyzed. [6] ) Symptoms also include foul-smelling, greasy stools, flatulence, abdominal cramps and bloating, nausea, and anorexia. The disease may be associated with dehydration as well. Extraintestinal symptoms, though rare, include hives, angioedema, eczema, erythema nodosum, reactive arthritides, dysuria, and ophthalmologic involvement. Most infections are self-limiting; however, a small percentage become chronic. Infections that clear usually will do so in about 13-30 days. [6] Classically, the patient's history will include drinking, swimming, or playing in unsterilized or contaminated water. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Giardiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Nearly half of the individuals infected with giardiasis are asymptomatic. The onset of symptoms is typically 1 to 2 weeks after infection for those with symptoms. Typical symptoms include abdominal pain, nausea, and flatulence, accompanied by large volume, watery, foul-smelling, and greasy stools. Children may present with abdominal pain with minimal diarrhea. Due to frequent loose stools, infected individuals often suffer from dehydration. [23] Fever is a less common symptom but can occur. In rare cases, patients might exhibit skin lesions and joint pain due to reactive arthritis. Symptoms typically resolve on their own within 4 weeks of onset. Individuals may present with weight loss and show signs or symptoms of vitamin deficiency in chronic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba coli Infection -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is symptomatic and no other organism or causes are found, treatment may be indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Giardia Lamblia Enteritis (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic symptoms include intermittent chronic diarrhea or loose stools, bloating, abdominal pain, functional dyspepsia, secondary lactose intolerance, malnutrition, and weight loss. Interestingly, there is evidence of chronic giardia infection in approximately 5% to 10% of patients diagnosed with inflammatory bowel syndrome (IBS). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Giardia, Cryptosporidium, and Entamoeba stay unaffected by water chlorination; therefore, suspicion for these parasites should be high in contaminated water. Also, there is an association between Campylobacter infection, agriculture, and drinking water. [19]

An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Gastritis and duodenitis is a gastrointestinal inflammatory condition that is classified as a specific manifestation within the broader category of الْتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis). It can give rise to or be related to الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ, indicating a more severe or erosive form of gastric inflammation. Clinically, gastritis and duodenitis has been associated with تَغَوُّطٌ مُدَمَّى, suggesting the presence of bloody stool and possible lower gastrointestinal bleeding, and with فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي, indicating that patients who are HIV‑positive may be at increased risk for or present with this condition. Recognizing these relationships assists clinicians in evaluating disease severity, considering underlying immunocompromise, and selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1796
retrieved_docs.content: Noninfectious gastroenteritis is the English designation for the condition referred to in Arabic as داء المتشاخسات; it describes inflammation of the gastrointestinal tract that is not caused by infectious agents. Recognizing that داء المتشاخسات maps to noninfectious gastroenteritis facilitates accurate cross‑language communication, proper diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients presenting with non‑infectious gastrointestinal inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis is categorized under the broader entity الِتِهابُ المَعِدَة (gastritis) and includes the more specific subtype الِتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ (erosive gastritis). Recognizing that the symptom complex may reflect both the general gastritis condition and its erosive form aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis and targeted management.
